IS THE IPO PROCESS A SCAM?
articles.summary: Figma's stock popped 250% on IPO day - the highest since 1999. For those who weren't following, this wasn't actually mispricing, but artificial scarcity at work. Only 3% of shares were available to trade because investment banks deliberately keep the float tiny. Long-term investors got their allocation and held tight while traders scrambled for the few shares available. Within days, the stock crashed 50% as reality set in. Now it's easy to call IPOs a scam, but this is exactly how the system is designed to work - banks manufacture these pops to make headlines and reward their best clients with instant profits.

articles.title: META ACQUIRES AI AUDIO STARTUP WAVEFORMS
articles.summary: Meta has acquired AI startup WaveForms, bolstering its AI unit, Superintelligence Labs. Co-founders Alexis Conneau and Coralie Lemaitre have joined Meta. WaveForms focused on the "Speech Turing Test" and "Emotional General Intelligence."

articles.title: CHATGPT IS BRINGING BACK 4O AS AN OPTION BECAUSE PEOPLE MISSED IT
articles.summary: OpenAI has reinstated GPT-4o as an option in ChatGPT following backlash from users mourning its replacement with GPT-5. Users missed GPT-4o's perceived personality and flexibility for various tasks. OpenAI indicated that Plus users could choose between models. CEO Sam Altman also stated OpenAI aims to address concerns over GPT-5's performance and transparency.

articles.title: HOW TO 'TEST' YOUR MESSAGING TO FIND MESSAGE-MARKET FIT
articles.summary: Message-Market Fit means your ideal buyers immediately "get it". You see high response rates on outbound, better click-through rates, and prospects understand your value before you explain it. To test properly, run 2-3 meaningfully different messaging angles across channels you're already using, aim for at least 8-12 qualified leads per variant over two weeks, then debrief with sales to see which message got people talking with fewer objections - if one variant improves conversion by 15%+, double down and bake it into all your GTM assets.

articles.title: MILLIONS IN REVENUE WITH A <15 PERSON TEAM
articles.summary: Most startups die because they build what they think customers want, not what customers actually need. Vitalize Care's founders slept in hospitals for eight months watching nurses work - and discovered their mental health app was solving the wrong problem. Nurses didn't need therapy, they needed someone to fix their staffing nightmare. After throwing out their entire product and selling hospitals on Figma mockups, they hit millions in ARR with just 15 people. Now, every engineer flies to hospitals monthly because that extreme customer proximity is their superpower.

articles.title: SHOULD WE OFFER PILOTS?
articles.summary: A Chief Procurement Officer insists that not offering pilots is a "major red flag." However, the best sales teams do something counterintuitive - they default to no pilots and make buyers pay to prove they're serious. If you must offer one, take a page from CJ Edmonds at Conversion Logix, who structures pilots with continuation clauses that auto-convert to paid contracts unless cancelled within 120 days. Most buyers requesting pilots aren't actually testing functionality - they're testing whether you're enterprise-grade. So if you must offer one, define success metrics upfront, charge for it, and remember that pilots should be your last resort.

articles.title: THE PRIVATE MARKET TRAP
articles.summary: Money is flowing into private companies but can't get out - there's $13 trillion trapped. You should care because startups convince people to work for less money by giving them stock. That deal only makes sense if the stock can turn into cash someday. No IPOs means that stock is just paper, which means talented people may be less inclined to join startups.

articles.title: EUROPE'S GREAT FOUNDERS MUST UNRETIRE
articles.summary: European entrepreneurs tend to retire early, missing potential decades of innovation and productivity. This contrasts with the US, where cultural norms push founders to create continually, contributing significantly to technological advancements. Europe must encourage its most successful entrepreneurs to keep building, or it risks falling behind the US and China in technology and innovation.

articles.title: HOW DEBT TO EBITDA RATIOS ARE IMPACTING DEALS IN 2025
articles.summary: Everyone obsesses over valuation multiples, but the metric that actually controls private equity deals is simpler: how much debt you can pile on. In 2021, PE firms were loading companies with 7.5x EBITDA in debt, and someone would always fund it. Today, you're lucky to get 5x, and junior debt has essentially vanished. The entire PE model depends on leverage to juice returns - when debt dries up, so do the deals. That's why take-privates of companies like PagerDuty and Olo are suddenly everywhere. With rates high and lenders spooked, PE firms can only make the math work on deeply discounted public companies.
